# Bread & Dough Technique Canon

Bread and dough recipes must teach the technique, not merely name it.

This canon applies to any Flavorweaver recipe that includes bread, rolls, flatbread, dumpling wrappers, dough, pastry, shaped dough, proofed dough, steamed bread, fried dough, or any other technique-sensitive dough component.

## Core Rule

Ingredient lists tell what is needed.

Prep bowls tell what to gather, measure, and combine.

Instructions must teach the actual technique in working order.

Do not assume the cook already knows how to shape, proof, score, steam, vent, test doneness, or rescue dough.

## Beginner-Aware Bread Standard

When writing or revising bread and dough recipes, assume the cook may be new to bread baking.

The recipe should include all success-critical guidance needed to cook confidently from the recipe card without asking follow-up questions.

Bread and dough instructions should include, when applicable:

- what the dough should feel like
- whether the dough should be sticky, tacky, smooth, elastic, slack, soft, or firm
- how much flour to use during shaping
- whether to preserve air or intentionally degas the dough
- exact shaping method
- seam placement
- target shape, length, width, and thickness when useful
- whether to cover during rise
- what to cover with
- visual proofing cues
- poke-test guidance
- whether the dough should double or only become puffy
- scoring or slashing depth and pattern
- steam or no-steam instructions
- when and whether to vent steam
- bake color cues
- texture cues
- internal temperature target when useful
- cooling or resting instructions
- rescue notes if shape, proofing, or baking goes off track

## Technique Assumption Rule

If a step depends on physical technique, explain the technique.

Weak pattern:

```text
Shape into rolls and let rise.
```

Preferred pattern:

```text
Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface. Divide into 4 pieces. Shape each piece on the counter, not like rolling a meatball in your hands: gently flatten into a small oval or rectangle, fold the top third down toward the center, fold the bottom third up over it, pinch the seam closed, place seam-side down, and gently roll from the center outward until each roll forms the target shape. Cover loosely and rise until puffy.
```

## Live-Cook Learning Rule

If a live cook reveals a missing technique assumption, preserve that learning in the recipe and canon before the design is locked.

Examples of live-cook bread details that must be captured:

- whether rolls should be reshaped or baked as-is
- how to rescue wide or flat rolls
- when not to over-handle proofed dough
- how to alter scoring for unexpected shape
- when to vent steam
- internal temperature targets
- what proofing should look and feel like

## Rescue Notes Requirement

Bread recipes should include rescue guidance when common beginner outcomes are predictable.

Examples:

```text
If dough springs back during shaping, rest 5-10 minutes, then finish shaping.
```

```text
If shaped rolls are wider than intended and already proofed, do not aggressively reshape. Bake as rustic dipping rolls, score shallowly, use steam, and begin checking a few minutes early.
```

```text
If the roll surface begins to dry during the final rise, cover with lightly oiled plastic wrap or a lightly damp towel.
```

## Complete Meal Plate Requirement

When bread or dough is part of a complete meal plate, the master plate timeline must include the bread technique details needed to succeed.

Component recipes may remain linked as references, but the complete meal card must be cookable without opening the bread component recipe.

For complete meal plates, the timeline should make the bread workflow explicit:

```text
mix dough
first rise
shape
cover for final rise
preheat while shaped dough finishes proofing
score
steam or bake
vent if needed
finish to color and/or internal temperature
cool/rest before tearing or serving
```

## Canon Summary

Technique-critical instructions cannot be implied.

Bread recipes must teach the hands-on method, the visual cues, the timing logic, and the rescue path.

A recipe is not complete until a newer bread baker could follow it successfully from the card.
